Then, they realized something else was afoot.

New Delhi has some of the world’s most polluted air. But how harmful it is can depend on how wealthy you are.
@dwtkns explains how The New York Times documented how much polluted air two children from different backgrounds were exposed to in one day.

The resulting story aimed to strike a balance between science and journalism. It used hard measurements to back up more traditional, on-the-ground reporting to take a visual look at how an often invisible threat affects two children’s lives.
The team included @dwtkns, a Times graphics editor, and involved more than a dozen journalists working closely with @gettleman, The Times’s South Asia bureau chief. https://nyti.ms/3nNT5d1

With two videography teams and portable air monitors, we measured the pollution that Monu, 13, and Aamya, 11, were exposed to moment-by-moment, showing them in parallel as they went about a normal day. https://nyti.ms/3nNT5d1

From the start of their day, Dec. 3 of last year, the contrasts in exposure were striking. Aamya was driven to school in an air conditioned car. Monu, from a less wealthy family, rode his bike through the smog. https://nyti.ms/3nNT5d1
The children’s schools offered more contrast: Monu’s was outdoors under a busy and polluted highway; Aamya’s was indoors at a school with air filters. https://nyti.ms/3nNT5d1

At home, Monu’s mother cooked meals outdoors over an open flame. Aamya’s family employed a cook. All the while, we measured the pollution in the air to document differences in the kids’ exposure. https://nyti.ms/3nNT5d1

The results were telling: Monu was exposed to about four times as much pollution as Aamya on the day we were with them, a disparity that could have dire consequences for his health if it continued throughout his life.

The Trump administration did have positive stories to tell: Operation Warp Speed helped drive the pharmaceutical industry’s progress in developing vaccines. By the end of the year, two highly effective treatments would be approved for emergency use. https://nyti.ms/3o4vJ2Y

As the gap between Trump’s political priorities and the realities of the pandemic widened, infighting among administration officials and scientific advisers intensified, leading to confusion and policy paralysis as thousands of Americans died. https://nyti.ms/3o4vJ2Y
